Event: Jack enlists Kate to stitch his back wound after the crash
Explanation: He secures needed medical help and forges a bond, while Kate gains purpose and they both benefit.

Event: Jack clings to denial after literally seeing the Island disappear
Explanation: His refusal to accept reality worsens his delusion and impairs his ability to help anyone.

Event: Jack starts drinking heavily and abusing prescription medication
Explanation: The coping attempt destroys his own health and relationships and doesn’t help anyone else.

Event: Jack forges his father’s signature to write himself prescriptions
Explanation: Illegally feeding his addiction risks his career and harms institutional trust without real benefit.

Event: Jack tries to amputate Boone’s leg in a near-hopeless attempt to save him
Explanation: His savior complex drives an extreme, likely futile procedure that drains him and worsens Boone’s condition.

Event: Jack grows a “beard of sorrow” while sinking into post‑Island depression
Explanation: His neglectful behavior manifests his psychological collapse, harming himself while not affecting others much.

Event: Jack shaves his beard once Ben gives him a renewed purpose
Explanation: Regaining purpose helps his own functioning and makes him more effective for Ben’s plans.

Event: Jack stalks his ex‑wife after being unable to let go
Explanation: His obsessive behavior deepens his own dysfunction and creates distress for her.

Event: Jack opens his eye and immediately runs around pulling crash survivors from the wreckage
Explanation: He endangers himself to save several lives and quickly organizes effective aid.

Event: Jack postpones treating his own serious side wound until others are safe
Explanation: He worsens his own injury to ensure others’ safety and stability.

Event: Jack coins and delivers his “Live together, die alone” speech
Explanation: The speech strengthens group cohesion and leadership while also reinforcing his own authority and purpose.

Event: Jack accepts the unwanted leadership role over the 815 survivors
Explanation: Though burdensome, his leadership improves group survival and order.

Event: Jack later learns to pass the leadership torch (to Hurley) when appropriate
Explanation: Relinquishing control relieves his burden and empowers a more suitable leader, benefitting everyone.

Event: Jack agrees to Jacob’s offer and becomes guardian of the Island
Explanation: He gains clarity of purpose and protects the Island and its people at great effort and risk.

Event: Jack donates his own blood to Boone as a universal donor
Explanation: The transfusion harms his own health and, as it only pools uselessly, fails to help Boone.

Event: Jack stops the transfusion to prevent further drain on himself
Explanation: He protects his own health by withdrawing a futile effort, at the cost of giving up on Boone’s slim chance.

Event: Jack testifies truthfully that his father’s drunken error killed a pregnant patient
Explanation: He sacrifices his relationship and his father’s career for the broader good of honesty and patient safety.

Event: Jack walks to the bamboo grove and chooses to face death calmly after saving the Island
Explanation: He accepts death with peace while ensuring his friends’ freedom and safety.

Event: Jack starts a relationship, then marries former patient Sarah out of obligation
Explanation: An imbalanced caretaker‑based marriage leaves him emotionally unfulfilled and collapses, hurting both.

Event: Jack saves Sarah instead of Adam Rutherford, indirectly causing Boone and Shannon to be on Flight 815
Explanation: He benefits Sarah greatly but unknowingly dooms Adam and sets in motion Boone and Shannon’s later deaths.

Event: Young Jack intervenes when bullies attack his friend Marc Silverman
Explanation: He suffers a beating but successfully protects his friend, a net gain for both in loyalty and safety.

Event: Jack beats Ethan in their rainy rematch fight
Explanation: Neutral‑to‑modest personal cost yields a clear gain in safety for others by defeating a threat.

Event: Jack fights the Smoke Monster in mortal form on the cliffs and is fatally wounded
Explanation: He sacrifices his life in the battle, fatally losing, but critically reduces the threat to everyone else.

Event: Jack becomes consumed with guilt after Locke’s death and his failure to save everyone
Explanation: His internal self‑punishment harms him while not materially improving others’ situations.

Event: Jack deliberately detonates a hydrogen bomb (Jughead) to try to erase Flight 815’s crash
Explanation: His extreme gamble fails and creates the very chain of events he meant to prevent, harming many.

Event: Jack champions Daniel’s plan to blow up Jughead in 1977
Explanation: He doubles down on a plan that destabilizes time and endangers the very people he wants to save.

Event: Jack refuses to operate on teenage Ben’s gunshot wound in 1977
Explanation: He spares himself ethical conflict but helps push Ben to the Others, worsening future dangers.

Event: Jack repeatedly beats Ben after believing he killed Bernard, Jin, and Sayid
Explanation: The rage beating brings no strategic gain, harms Jack physically/emotionally, and does little to help others.

Event: Jack tackles Locke and attempts to shoot him for killing Naomi
Explanation: Driven by anger and a desire for revenge, he risks himself and group cohesion without helping others.

Event: Jack returns to the Island, disrupting Sawyer and Juliet’s stable life
Explanation: His return upends their happiness and contributes to Juliet’s death while further burdening him.

Event: Jack smashes all the Lighthouse mirrors upon realizing Jacob has spied on him
Explanation: Acting out of anger, he destroys a useful tool and gains no real benefit.

Event: Jack keeps Charlie’s withdrawal secret, telling Hurley it’s the flu
Explanation: He protects Charlie’s dignity and gains trust while still treating the real problem.

Event: Jack assumes self‑appointed leadership of the survivors early on
Explanation: His initiative stabilizes the group and gives him a meaningful role.

Event: Jack ends Edward Mars’s life to spare him a slow, painful death
Explanation: He accepts moral and emotional burden to relieve Mars’s suffering.

Event: Jack initially refuses to operate on Ben but later does so to gain leverage
Explanation: He uses Ben’s dependence on him strategically, advancing his bargaining position and others’ safety.

Event: Jack refuses to operate on Ben’s 1977 gunshot, forcing Kate and Sawyer to take Ben to the Others
Explanation: His refusal indirectly strengthens the Others and Ben’s future power, backfiring on everyone.

Event: Jack calls for “rescue,” which instead brings Keamy’s mercenaries
Explanation: His attempt to escape results in violent mercenaries arriving and many deaths.

Event: Jack beats Ben almost to death after Ben claims to have executed his friends
Explanation: He vents rage that neither helps his friends nor improves his own position, while risking injury and moral fallout.

Event: Jack sabotages Sawyer’s recovery by threatening to halt antibiotics unless he surrenders the briefcase
Explanation: He weaponizes Sawyer’s medical dependence for leverage, risking Sawyer’s health for his own objectives.

Event: Jack uses his knowledge to troll Sawyer with humiliating STD questions
Explanation: He amuses himself and diagnoses the problem at the cost of Sawyer’s dignity and trust.

Event: Jack burns the bodies from the crash while initially waiting for rescue
Explanation: The action helps with sanitation and closure, preparing the group while awaiting help.

Event: Jack moves some survivors to the inland caves once he realizes rescue isn’t coming
Explanation: Relocation improves long‑term survival prospects and living conditions for the group.

Event: Jack attempts to hang onto his failed marriage and is unable to let it go
Explanation: His refusal to accept the end prolongs mutual pain and entrenches his own issues.

Event: Jack repeatedly tries to “fix” everything despite consistently worsening outcomes
Explanation: His unchecked savior complex often escalates crises for others while burdening himself.

Event: Jack accepts that he can’t kill himself and uses this to convince Richard not to commit suicide
Explanation: He leverages his “plot armor” insight to restore Richard’s will to live and purpose while clarifying his own.

Event: Jack lights a stick of dynamite next to himself and Richard and waits as the fuse burns out
Explanation: He risks his own life to demonstrate Jacob’s protection, renewing Richard’s faith and motivation.

Event: Jack later tries to repeat the bomb‑fuse trick with the Man in Black’s bomb
Explanation: The misjudged move, combined with Sawyer’s interference, leads to disaster and deaths.

Event: Jack manipulates Ben, Juliet, and Tom to save Kate and Sawyer
Explanation: He increases his strategic leverage to rescue allies with minimal lasting harm to himself.

Event: Jack gives a hardline speech to coerce Sawyer into relinquishing the gun briefcase
Explanation: He secures weapons and control for himself and the camp by threatening Sawyer’s health.

Event: Jack grows into a supportive, loving father to David in the flash‑sideways
Explanation: Healing the generational trauma helps his own peace and greatly benefits his son’s well‑being.

Event: Jack goes to David’s recital and tells him he’ll always be proud of him
Explanation: This explicit emotional support strengthens their bond and helps Jack reconcile with his own paternal wounds.

Event: Jack stays alive despite mortal wounds to appoint Hurley as his successor
Explanation: He uses his last strength to empower Hurley’s leadership, sacrificing his remaining life for others’ future.

Event: Jack frees Desmond from the Heart and reseals it himself
Explanation: He expends the last of his life ensuring Desmond’s safety and the Island’s stability.

Event: Jack and Kate share a final kiss before he returns to the Heart to die
Explanation: This moment of closure comforts them both and strengthens Kate before her escape.

Event: Jack ends up with Kate in the primary timeline
Explanation: Their mutual choice provides emotional fulfillment without clear harm to others.

Event: Jack comforts Sawyer indirectly by relaying Christian’s message of pride (via Sawyer’s story)
Explanation: Accepting his father’s posthumous approval helps his own healing and improves his attitude toward others.

Event: Jack smiles as he dies after seeing his friends escape on the Ajira plane
Explanation: Knowing they’re safe gives him emotional closure while confirming their survival and freedom.

Event: Jack accepts that Locke “was right” too late and lives with that regret
Explanation: His recognition brings personal pain but clarifies the truth that influences his later, more selfless choices.

Event: Jack repeatedly cries and shows vulnerability instead of repressing it
Explanation: Expressing emotion helps his own processing and deepens bonds with others who see his humanity.

Event: Jack snarks and trolls his friends when in a good mood (e.g., with Sawyer)
Explanation: Light teasing builds camaraderie and eases tension without causing real harm.
